The dog is on his side in the patch of sun by the door, doing that thing where he breathes so deeply he sounds like he's dreaming. The novel is on the table where I put it Thursday, two days ago now, still waiting. Theo is upstairs in the particular sleep of a Saturday teenager.

I told myself last week I'd try the two hours. The permission. The nothing it's supposed to do.

Today might be the day.

entries.prompt: What's the last time someone needed something from you that you genuinely didn't have to give — time, attention, energy, whatever it was? Walk me through the moment you realized it, and what you did next.
entries.content:
Mom called on Thursday at two-fifteen, which is when she always calls — not always, but often enough that I've started to notice the pattern, the particular way the phone buzzes in the early afternoon like it knows I've been sitting still long enough to pick up. I was in the middle of something real. Not the outreach document, not the rearranging-headers thing — actually in it, the third block, a section that had been soft for two weeks and was finally starting to develop some density. The kind of moment where the wrong interruption takes twenty minutes to recover from.

I picked up.

She sounded fine. She usually sounds fine. We have the shape of it now, her and I — she asks about the kids, I give her the updates, she asks about Jamie and I say tired but good, she laughs at something, we do the pause that means she's coming to the thing she actually called about. This time the thing was her furnace. Not a crisis — it was making a sound, a technician had already been out, the technician had said it was fine. She was calling me to tell me the technician had said it was fine.

We talked for twenty-five minutes.

I don't know exactly when I realized I had nothing left to give the conversation. It wasn't one moment. More like a slow draining — I was there for the furnace update, there for the technician story, there when she mentioned the neighbor's tree again (the neighbor's tree has been a recurring subject since September, I know more about this tree than I know about some of my own concerns), and then somewhere around the fifteen-minute mark I noticed I was looking at the cursor blinking in the document and the distance between what I was doing on the phone and the thing I'd been doing before was suddenly very wide. Like I'd crossed from one room into another and the door had closed quietly behind me.

She asked if everything was okay with me. She does this — at least once per call, usually around minute twelve, she asks as if she's checking the pilot light. I said yes, everything is good, the workshop is coming together. She made the warm sound. The relieved sound. I could hear her settling back in it. And what I was feeling, at that precise moment, was something I wouldn't say to anyone: *I have nothing left for this call and I have been here for fifteen minutes and we are probably ten minutes from done and I just have to get through it.*

That's not a good feeling to have about your mother.

I know why it happened. She was not asking for much. She was asking for twenty-five minutes and a warm presence and some reassurance that the people she worries about are fine. That's not a large request. But I'd been in the third block for two hours, the real kind of in-it, and there's a quality of attention that doesn't subdivide — you're either doing it or you're not, and when she called I was doing it. The twenty-five minutes were not twenty-five minutes from a full tank. They were twenty-five minutes from the specific remainder you have after two hours of close work, which is not the same.

What I did was stay on the phone and not say any of this.

I listened to the rest of the furnace story. I asked a question about the neighbor's tree that I knew would produce another three minutes of tree, which it did, which gave me something to make sounds about without having to generate content. I said I was glad the technician said it was fine. I said I'd come up in the next few weeks. She said that would be lovely. We wrapped up.

After I put the phone down I sat at the table for a few minutes without opening the document. The dog came over and I scratched his ear. The cursor was still blinking.

The thing is I couldn't tell her. There is no version of that conversation where I say: *Mom, I'm in the middle of something, can I call you back* that doesn't arrive in her chest as something to worry about. I have tried earlier versions of this and what happens is that she says of course, yes, go, don't let me keep you — and then the rest of the call is her trying to get off the phone faster than I've asked, and I spend the next hour feeling like I've done something unkind. It's easier to stay on the phone. That's a calculation I run automatically now, without deciding, except that on Thursday the calculation had a cost I felt more than usual.

I've been wondering since whether the cost is the honest accounting or just the mood of the afternoon. If I'd had a worse day in the document, would I have noticed the draining at all, or would the call have been the relief? Probably the latter. The attention I didn't have to give on Thursday is the same attention I sometimes have in surplus, on the slower days, the ones where I would have stayed on longer if she'd let me.

*The call doesn't change. You change.*

Yes. That's the thing I keep turning over. She's asking for the same thing she's always asking for. I'm the variable. And on the days when I have it, the call is fine — easy, even, sometimes genuinely good. On the days when I'm deep in something, it costs in a way that leaves a small deposit of guilt I carry for the rest of the afternoon.

I did get back to the document. Not immediately — I made another coffee first, moved around the kitchen for a few minutes, let the transition happen. Sat back down. The section came back to me more quickly than I expected. I added a full paragraph, read it back, kept most of it. The afternoon recovered.

But I'm still thinking about the moment on the phone, the cursor blinking, the not having anything left. Not because I did something wrong — I stayed on the call, I was warm, she doesn't know what was happening on my end of the line. But because I notice I've started to budget her calls the way I budget certain kinds of tasks. Not consciously, not on a spreadsheet. Just — a background awareness that if she calls when I'm mid-way through something real, there's an exchange happening, and the exchange is one I've agreed to without ever having been asked.

I am the on-call one. That was true before I left the old life and it's true now and it would be true if I had stayed. It's not a consequence of the choice, it's just a fact of the family geometry, the way certain things settle. Dani is across the country. I am an hour away. The hour is the geometry.

What I didn't have to give on Thursday was the specific quality of present attention. I gave her a different grade of the thing instead. She didn't know the difference.

I'm not sure that's fine. I'm not sure it's not fine either.

The workshop is in two weeks. The introduction is written. The third block is almost there.

She'll call again next week. I'll pick up.

entries.prompt: What's something you're looking forward to that you haven't told anyone about yet — walk me through what you're imagining when you let yourself think about it.
entries.content:
The dog has been in and out three times already this morning and it's not yet seven, which means the squirrel situation by the fence is ongoing. I've stopped getting up to look. He'll figure it out or he won't.

There's something I've been carrying around for about a week that I haven't said to anyone. Not to Jamie, not to Pat, not in here until now. It's not dramatic. It's the opposite of dramatic, which might be why it's stayed quiet — it's too small to say out loud without qualifying it into nothing, and I've been wanting to have it unqualified for a little while longer.

What I keep imagining is the moment after the workshop ends.

Not during. Not the introduction, which is settled now, two sentences that tell the truth and stop. Not the second block or the third paragraph or the handoffs with Pat or whether the room will have the energy I'm planning for. I've spent enough time inside all of that. What I keep coming back to is the ten or fifteen minutes after. The room clearing. People putting on coats, talking in pairs, the particular movement of a crowd that has been sitting and is now moving. And somewhere in that — I can see it clearly enough that it has a specific texture — someone comes over and says something. Not necessarily praise. Not necessarily anything I can predict the content of. Just: someone who was in that room finds their way to me and says the thing that tells me something landed.

I've been imagining this in the way you're not supposed to imagine things before they happen. Specifically. With detail. The quality of light in the room — I've never been to the venue, I only know it fits thirty, so I've constructed a composite room from other rooms I've stood in, with windows on one side and chairs arranged in that particular in-between way that says this is a workshop and not a lecture. The way I'll feel in the twenty minutes before it starts, which will not be good — I know this about myself, the pre-room feeling is not something I've managed to make pleasant, it's just something I move through. And then the after. The coats, the pairs, the someone.

I don't know why I haven't said this to Jamie.

Actually, I think I do. Jamie would receive it carefully, the way Jamie receives everything — the genuine support and the quiet inventory-taking happening at the same time — and then ask a good question, something like: what specifically do you want them to have gotten out of it? Which is an excellent question and one I've been asking myself and it would be the right question and it would also immediately move the conversation from the imagined thing to the work of preparing for the imagined thing. Which is the right move. It's the helpful move. But the imagining itself, the thing I've been doing in the early mornings when I let myself think forward to the week after the workshop — that would close a little. Jamie would have it now, and then it would be a conversation, and then it would be a plan.

I want to keep having it for a few more days.

What I'm imagining underneath the room-clearing moment — the real thing, the thing behind the thing — is what it would mean for how I think about the rest of the year. Not in a strategic way. More like proof of concept. There are seven versions of an outreach document in a folder and one email sent and the gap between what I can describe and what I can demonstrate has been the reason I keep almost-starting. The workshop is the first thing I will have done that is entirely new-life, no title behind it, no organization doing quiet work in the background. If something lands in that room, I will have demonstrated something to myself that I cannot demonstrate by rearranging headers.

*This is the thing you've been building toward for nine months and you haven't said it out loud because saying it makes it possible to fail at it specifically.*

Yes. That's the part I'm sitting with this morning. The imagining is not just hope, it's also a kind of exposure — because I'm seeing clearly enough what I want from that afternoon that I now know what not getting it would feel like. Before I let myself imagine it, the workshop was just the next concrete thing. Now it has a shape I'm attached to. That's a different situation.

The outreach person replied. Four days after I sent, then nothing for another week, and then two days ago: short, warm, mentioned that they'd been meaning to reconnect. No ask on their end yet, nothing concrete, but the door is not closed. I read the message twice and closed it and went for a walk and didn't tell anyone. I'm not sure why I'm telling this here and not in a separate entry — maybe because it's in the same register, the things I'm keeping close for a little while before they become conversations.

The novel is still on the table. I read forty pages of it on Sunday — not the two hours I said I'd try for, but forty pages in the morning light before anyone needed anything, with the dog at my feet and nothing it was supposed to do. Forty pages of a novel that is very good and that I will finish, slowly, in the way I used to finish things. The chair I had is gone but the kitchen table at six-forty in the morning is doing something similar. I'm noting this.

Jamie comes down in about twenty minutes, I'd guess from the sounds upstairs. Nina will be up shortly after, with an agenda and questions that go straight to the point. Theo on a Sunday will sleep until the house produces enough noise to be worth investigating, which is usually mid-morning.

I'm going to have another twenty minutes of the imagining before the day starts making requests.

The room after the workshop. The coats going on. Thirty people who came because they wanted something, and whether or not I helped them find it. Pat on the other side of the room talking to someone, and the particular thing it will mean to look across that space at Pat and have that conversation with my eyes that says: we did this, it was real, let's see what happens next.

And somewhere in it, maybe, the sound that means something landed.

I'm going to let myself have that for eleven more days. Then I'll be in the room and the imagining will either have been close to true or it won't, and either way the prep work is almost done and the introduction is settled and the third paragraph is good and I have been building something for nine months that is starting to feel like it has a shape.

The dog has given up on the squirrel. He's back inside, asleep under the table, breathing his big morning breathing.

The room is quiet. I'm going to sit here a while longer.

entries.prompt: What's something you said recently that landed differently than you meant it — walk me through the moment you realized it, and what you did after?
entries.content:
The kettle's been sitting on the burner long enough that it's lost some temperature, which means the coffee is going to be slightly off, which is exactly the kind of small thing that accumulates into a mood if I let it. I'm not letting it.

Wednesday night. We were at dinner and Nina was doing the thing where she reports the full contents of her school day in real time, complete with character assessments of everyone involved, and Theo was eating with the focused attention of someone who finds the table negotiable and is deciding whether to stay. And I said something about the workshop — someone asked, Nina I think, because she asks about the workshop roughly as often as she asks what my job is now, which is to say every few days with the consistency of a tide chart — and I said we're in good shape, I think it's going to go really well.

Not a strange thing to say. True enough. The introduction is settled, the arc holds, I'm not bracing against it the way I was three weeks ago. Fine.

And then I said: *I'm actually kind of excited about it.*

I haven't said that before. Not to anyone, not in those words. The thing I've been carrying around in the quiet of the early mornings — the imagined room, the coats going on afterward, the particular thing I want to feel when it's done — I have been keeping that specifically because it's too exposed, too attached, and saying it out loud makes it possible to fail at it in a particular way. And then I said I was excited about it, at the dinner table, to my family, over pasta.

Jamie looked up. Good look, warm. Nina said "that's good, right?" in the tone she has when she's checking whether a thing is a good-news item or a complicated-news item. Theo — and this is the part I'm still sitting with — Theo looked at me for a second and said, "you've been working on it forever."

Not unkind. Factual. Theo does not do unkind very fluently. But the way he said it — *forever* — had something in it that I read, maybe incorrectly, as an accounting. Like the duration of the preparation had registered. Like it had been long enough to notice.

I said something like yeah, it's been a real process, and moved on, and Nina picked the thread back up in her direction and the table continued. But I spent the rest of dinner hearing the word *forever* in the particular frequency where you know it landed somewhere you didn't intend to send it.

What I mean is this: I thought I was sharing something small. I thought I was just being present at the table, saying the true thing, letting my family know I'm not dreading the thing I've been preparing for. That was the intention. What I said instead — heard from far enough back to see the shape of it — was: *this has taken a long time and I have been in here with it and you have all been watching me be in here with it, and now I am telling you I feel something about it.* That is not the same thing as sharing something small.

I put dishes in the sink and thought about Theo's word for most of the clearing-up.

The uncomfortable version of what I think happened is that *forever* is accurate. The workshop has been the organizing fact of this house for six weeks, or some aspect of it has been present in the house — the door closed, the document open, the calls with Pat, the mornings where I'm in the kitchen early and visibly working something out. I think of it as my project, my preparation. I have not fully thought about what it looks like from inside a house where it's just the ambient weather of having a parent who is doing a thing.

Theo would not have said that two years ago. Two years ago I was doing a different kind of thing, which also had its own ambient weather. The difference is that I wasn't here to hear his version of it. Now I'm here and I hear the *forever*, and I don't know whether to be glad I'm in the house enough to receive it or to feel the particular guilt of someone who thought the being-here was going cleanly in one direction.

I didn't say anything else about it that night.

What I did was go downstairs after the kids were up in their rooms, sit at this table, and do the thing where I replay a conversation looking for the seam I missed. Found it pretty quickly: I said *excited* like I was reporting a weather condition and didn't account for the possibility that my family has been watching me not-say-it for six weeks and that my saying it now means something different than just the word. Like the word *excited* was lit from behind by everything that came before it and I couldn't see that because I was the one holding the light.

There's also the Pat situation, which I keep circling back to. I said *excited* at the dinner table and couldn't say it to Pat in eleven days of messaging and voice memos and a full working-through of every structural element of the thing. Pat and I talk more freely than almost anyone in my current life and there are still whole rooms I don't open. And yet apparently I'll say the word over pasta when Nina asks.

Maybe what I'm actually sitting with is that the people who live inside the same walls as the thing don't get the curated version. They get the weather. And sometimes the weather says something I didn't know I was saying.

Thursday morning I asked Theo, on the drive to school, how things were. I do this periodically and the results are variable. He said fine. I said anything coming up this week that's stressful. He thought about it — I could see him deciding whether the question was worth the investment — and said there was a test, kind of annoying. I said yeah. He looked out the window for the rest of the drive.

Not a breakthrough. I've been trained out of expecting breakthrough-shaped things. But it was the question, offered and received without either of us pretending the conversation was something other than what it was. That felt like something adjacent to the right direction.

The workshop is in eleven days. The word *excited* is still sitting at the dinner table where I left it, and Theo's *forever* is sitting across from it, and between them is probably something true about what these nine months have looked like from inside this house, which is different from what they've looked like from inside my head.

I should go take the dog out before the day starts. The coffee, even off-temperature, is almost gone.

I'll think about Theo on the drive to school tomorrow. Whether there's something I want to say, or whether what's needed is just the question and the window and the rest of it left open.

entries.prompt: What's the last time you changed your mind about someone — not dramatically, just a small shift in how you see them? Walk me through what happened and what's different now.
entries.content:
Pat sent a photo yesterday. Not of anything workshop-related — a picture of a view from somewhere, a trail or a ridge, trees and some sky, the kind of thing that's genuinely beautiful and also slightly hard to respond to in a message thread. I sent back a single word. *Nice.* And then sat with the mild awkwardness of that for a few minutes, the way you do when someone has offered something personal and you've handed back a syllable.

That's not what I want to write about. But it's where this started.

What I've been thinking about since yesterday afternoon is Pat. Not the workshop Pat, not the voice-memo Pat, not even the coffee-in-the-loud-place Pat who put the cup down when I said *you want to know what I actually think*. The Pat who took a picture of a view and sent it without explanation to someone they were going to see in less than two weeks. That's a different data point than the ones I've been working with.

I've been building my version of Pat out of specific materials. The three years of doing this work that I don't have. The possible engagement Pat mentioned — bigger scope, real news — that produced the two-feelings-at-once response I'm still not proud of. The sequencing idea that I took into the document and made mine before handing the credit back. The speed of the replies. I have been carrying a Pat who is slightly ahead of me and I have been relating to that Pat, and I don't think I fully understood how much of the picture I was leaving out until yesterday.

The picture came in around four. I was at the table with the third block open, one of the maintenance passes, not the real kind of in-it but present enough. I looked at the photo for a second and sent the syllable and put the phone down. And then I picked it back up and looked at the photo again.

There are no other people in it. It's just the view. Which means Pat either went alone or went with someone who wasn't in the frame, and I realized I have almost no information about which of those is more likely. In a year of working alongside this person — coffee, calls, memos, the whole evolving fabric of whatever this is — I have very little sense of what Pat's days look like when Pat is not doing the work. I know the professional autobiography in reasonable detail. I know the ceiling that turned out to be a ceiling. I know the three years and the possible engagement and the way Pat thinks on its feet.

I don't know if Pat goes on trails alone.

*You have been competitive with a version of this person you assembled from the parts that were most useful to be competitive with.*

That came up somewhere between looking at the photo the first time and the second time, and it arrived with the particular flatness of something you already knew and kept not knowing. I've been watching Pat for evidence that Pat is further along, moving faster, accumulating proof before I do. I've been less interested in the parts of Pat that don't confirm that story.

What shifted — and it's small, I want to be accurate about how small — is that the photo made Pat harder to hold as a composite. A person who takes a picture of a view and sends it without context is not performing anything. They're just — somewhere, looking at something, thinking of someone. That's a different kind of person than the one I've been carrying around.

I sent a second message about an hour later. Said it looked like a good afternoon for it. Pat replied: *needed to get out of the document for a while.* Then: *you doing okay?*

I sat there for a second.

In a year of messages and memos I'm not sure Pat has asked that without it being adjacent to something workshop-related. How's the prep going, is the second block feeling settled, how are you feeling about the introduction — those questions I know how to answer. *You doing okay* with no context after a picture of a view is a different register. I wasn't sure I knew what to do with it.

I said yes, mostly. Getting there. Eleven days.

Pat sent back: *I keep thinking about that conversation. The one about the ceiling.*

I knew immediately which one. The loud coffee place, three weeks ago. The cup going down.

I said me too.

And then we talked for a few minutes in the way we sometimes talk — not about logistics, not about the workshop — and I noticed that I was not preparing for it, not running the half-sentences in advance, just actually in the conversation. And the version of Pat in the conversation was not the three-years-of-this, possible-engagement Pat. It was just Pat, who had needed to get out of the document, who went somewhere and took a photo and sent it without explanation.

I don't think Pat is less formidable than I thought. I don't think the three years have become two years. The shift is not about the ledger. It's something more like — I had been relating to an outline of a person and yesterday I got more of the interior, and the interior is not what I would have predicted from the outline.

There's something I want to be careful about here, which is the tendency to overcorrect. To swing from: Pat is ahead of me and I'm watching the gap, to: Pat is vulnerable and three-dimensional and we are actually just two people trying to figure something out. Both of those are partial. The competitive thing is real — it lives in me, not in Pat, I've known this for a while — and it doesn't go away because I saw a photo of a view and had a conversation that went somewhere unplanned. The math is still the math.

But I think I'd been using the composite version of Pat partly as a way to keep a certain amount of distance. If Pat is primarily the person who is three years ahead, that's a useful arrangement — it explains the watching, it explains the two-feelings, it means I don't have to sit too close to the possibility that what we've been building this year is something more like a real collaboration between two people who are, in different ways and at different paces, figuring out the same thing.

*That's closer to what's true, and it's going to require something different from you.*

Closer to what's true. Not all the way there, probably. But closer.

I haven't sent anything else since the conversation yesterday. Not sure I need to. The workshop is in eleven days and we'll be in the same room, which is its own kind of reckoning — the real version of what we are together is going to happen in front of thirty people and all the composites will either hold or they won't.

I made dinner last night without the phone near me and thought about the photo every so often. Just the view. The trees and the sky and nobody in the frame.

entries.prompt: What's the last time you were in a room where you felt like a slightly different version of yourself — not performing exactly, just off from the usual? Walk me through who else was there, how you were acting, and what that version of you wanted.
entries.content:
The furnace guy came yesterday to do the annual check-in, which I'd been meaning to schedule since September and finally called about last week after the Mom conversation. He was there for forty minutes, did what he came to do, made some notes on a form, and at the end he said everything looks good, you're set for the season. I said great, thanks. He asked what I did — I think because I was home at two on a Thursday and the house had the particular daytime quality of a place someone works in — and I said I was doing some consulting, leadership work, some facilitation. He nodded the way people nod when an explanation makes sufficient sense to not require more. We shook hands. He left.

I stood in the front hallway for a minute after the door closed.

Something had been off about the forty minutes. Not the furnace, not the guy — he was efficient and entirely fine. It was me. The version of me that had been in that house while he was there.

I'd been chattier than necessary. Not friendly, which I can do naturally in small doses, but chatty in the specific way of someone who has been alone in a house for most of the day and is slightly overcompensating for it when another person arrives. I asked about his van when he came in — I don't know why, I have no interest in vans — and I made a comment about the neighborhood when I walked him down to the furnace, something unnecessary about how the street floods when it rains hard, which is true and also completely irrelevant to why he was there. I offered coffee and when he said no thanks I asked if he was sure, which is a thing I do not do. I am not a he-sure-about-the-coffee person.

It was the being-alone-in-the-house version of me, which I don't encounter very often because there's usually enough ambient life — Jamie, the kids, the dog requiring something, the phone. But yesterday the dog was at the vet for his regular thing, Jamie was at work, Theo and Nina were in school, and I'd been in the third block all morning and then gone on a long walk and come back and eaten lunch standing at the counter and been in the house alone for maybe five hours before the furnace guy knocked. Which is not a long time by any reasonable measure. It just had a quality.

The version of me in that hallway wanted, I think, to be a person who was legibly embedded in something. That's the most accurate way I can say it. The furnace guy's question — what do you do — was not probing, it was just the small talk that fills the space while you're putting on your boots at the end of a service call. But the question hit a nerve it shouldn't have hit, which is the nerve that is still, nine months in, activated by the question of how to describe myself to a stranger in a way that doesn't feel like I'm reading from a document I haven't finished writing.

In the old life the answer was simple. You had the words, the words had weight, the weight was recognized. The furnace guy would have nodded differently. Not better — I'm not saying better — just with the quick settling of someone who has received information that slots easily into a known category.

*You miss being legible. You should probably deal with that.*

That came up on the walk, actually, before the furnace situation, and the furnace situation just illustrated it. Because I'd said consulting, leadership work, facilitation — all true things, the introduction-adjacent version — and the guy had nodded fine and I'd still been chatty about the van.

The version of me that wanted to be legible, that asked about the van and offered coffee twice: I don't dislike that person, exactly. He's just — looser than I usually am. More obviously needing something from the interaction. In the old life I had so many interactions in a given week that no single one bore much weight, which produced a kind of ease I mistook for confidence. What I had was volume. Spread the needing across enough encounters and any one of them can feel effortless.

Now there are fewer. Each one carries a little more freight.

I called Pat last night — we've been texting mostly but I wanted to actually talk — and I said almost none of this, which is fine, we had things to go through. We're ten days out. We talked about the physical arrangement of the room and whether to leave the chairs in rows or push them into a horseshoe, which Pat favors and I think might feel forced with a group we don't know yet. We agreed to go in and see what the room feels like and decide then. Practical. Good. The conversation I needed.

But somewhere in it Pat said, offhandedly, that there was a window in the early afternoon where a second potential participant might be joining, someone from a company Pat has been in conversation with for months. Not confirmed, might not happen, but possible. I said great, the more the better. Said it easily, moved on.

And then I was in the kitchen after we hung up and I thought: if that person is in the room and the thing I'm hoping happens actually happens — the room clearing, the coats, someone finding their way to me — then that moment will also be in front of a potential client for one of us or both of us. The afternoon I've been imagining in the quiet of the early mornings will not be private in the way I've been holding it. It will have stakes attached that I didn't put there.

That's fine. That's just how things work, I know. But it did something to the imagining. Made it slightly colder.

I think what I wanted from that imagined moment — the thing I haven't said to anyone — was something that wasn't about the next thing. Just: the room, the thing that landed, the evidence for myself that this is real. Not the lead, not the follow-up email, not the conversation with Pat about what it means for the engagement. Just the clean version of having done something that worked.

And now there might be someone in that room who changes the temperature of it before I've had a chance to be in it.

The dog was back by the time I went to pick up Theo. He looked exhausted from the vet in the way he always does, like the experience of being handled by strangers requires a full recovery. He slept with his head on my feet while I made dinner, which is not his usual spot. I let him stay there.

Theo came down after dinner and sat in the kitchen while I was finishing the cleanup, which he doesn't always do. He didn't say much. He had his phone but wasn't fully in it the way he usually is, phone at a slightly different angle, more present in the room than in the screen. I didn't make anything of it. We were just in the same room for a while, the way it sometimes happens now.

Before he went up he said: hope the thing goes well. Meaning the workshop.

I said thank you.

He nodded once and went upstairs.

The furnace is fine. I'm set for the season. The room has a window in the early afternoon that might have someone in it, and the version of me that shows up ten days from now is going to have to be the one that doesn't ask about the van.

entries.prompt: What's the last thing someone said to you that you've turned over more than once since — not because it was hurtful, just because it stuck? Walk me through when they said it, and why you think it's still with you.
entries.content:
Dani called Sunday evening, which is when Dani usually calls — the tail end of the weekend, after whatever the day has been, the hour when you can have a real conversation without one of you rushing off. The kids were already upstairs. Jamie was reading. I took the phone out to the back steps because the night was still warm enough and the dog needed a last walk around the yard anyway.

We talked for forty minutes, which is longer than usual for Dani and me. Usually it's twenty-five, warm and careful the way I said, the warmth real and the care also real. This one went longer because Dani had something going on with work — nothing catastrophic, just a decision she was turning over, whether to take on a thing that would require more than she had available. I mostly listened. Asked a few questions. Dani works through things by talking and I've known this since she was in her twenties; my job in those conversations is mostly not to jump to the conclusion before she gets there herself.

Near the end, after the work thing had resolved itself into something she could sit with for now, Dani asked about the workshop. I said it was close. Felt ready, or close to ready. She said something about how it was nice to hear that — that I'd sounded different lately, she said. More settled.

I said something like, yeah, I think things are starting to come together.

And Dani said: *you sound like yourself again.*

Then we talked for a few more minutes about Mom's furnace, which Dani had also heard about, and wrapped up, and I sat on the back steps for a while after and watched the dog do his slow investigation of the fence line in the dark.

*You sound like yourself again.*

I've been turning it over since Sunday and today is Wednesday and the workshop is in six days and I'm still turning it over.

The thing about the sentence is that it requires a before. You can only sound like yourself *again* if there was a period in which you didn't. And I knew what Dani meant — I think I knew immediately — but knowing what she meant and sitting with what she meant are different operations, and I've been doing the second one mostly without meaning to, in the margins of other things. During the walk Monday morning. Loading the dishwasher. Reading the novel for an hour on Sunday before Dani called, and then not reading it after.

Because here's what I keep arriving at: if the before is real, then it was real for a long time. Not just the last hard stretch of the old life, the quarters that accumulated, the distance that wasn't geographic. Longer. If I'm only now sounding like myself, then the version of me that was present in this house and in my life for most of the last decade was — not absent, not false — but some reduced version. Running on a portion of the bandwidth. And I don't know how to measure what that means for Theo and Nina and Jamie and, frankly, for Dani herself, who was across the country calling into whatever version of me answered the phone.

The voice that shows up in the margins: *she meant it as good news and you have turned it into an audit.*

True. Also: the audit is happening whether or not I wanted it to. Dani handed me a piece of information I didn't have before, which is that the difference is audible. I knew I felt different. I didn't know it was something you could hear from two thousand miles away on a Sunday phone call.

What I can't quite get a hold of is what to do with that information. There's a version where I let it be simply good — nine months of hard and uncertain work and I sound like myself again, take the win, carry it into the room in six days. And I want that version. I've been wanting it since Sunday.

But there's another version that keeps showing up underneath it, which is something about all the conversations Dani and I have had over the years where she probably heard the other version and didn't say anything. The careful warmth going in both directions. I was doing the condensed edition of myself for long enough that she had a baseline for it, and what she heard Sunday sounded different from that baseline, and she said so, and now I'm sitting with the full duration of the baseline.

I told Jamie about the call on Monday. Said Dani had some work stuff, seemed okay about it, we talked for a while. Didn't say the part about sounding like yourself again. I'm not sure why, exactly. Jamie would receive it well — Jamie has been receiving things well for nine months with remarkable steadiness — and I think part of me just wanted to hold the specific sentence for a few more days before it became a conversation. Before it acquired the gentle inventory-taking that happens when things like this become conversations with Jamie, which is not a criticism of Jamie, that quality is one of the things that keeps this house running. I just wanted to stay inside it first.

The thing I keep coming back to is Theo at the back door, weeks ago, that morning I almost said the thing I didn't say. The broader shoulders. The looking-at-nothing. I've been thinking about him as someone I'm learning to read, a new frequency I'm only able to hear because I'm in the house more. What Dani's sentence does is make me wonder what he was hearing from me for the years before I had a frequency worth reading. Not a damage inventory — I don't think I want to go there, or I don't think it would be useful. But something adjacent to it, quieter. A recognition that *being home* and *being here* are not the same thing, and that the first was possible in the old life in a way the second wasn't, and that the difference between the two is something I probably won't ever have full clarity on.

*The version of you that existed before this year is not entirely gone. It's just quieter now than the other one.*

That came up on Monday's walk, which was long, the October-into-November light going flat and gray in a way that's become its own kind of beautiful to me. Not an accusation. Just something I was trying to figure out whether to believe.

Six days. The introduction is two sentences and they're the right two sentences. The third paragraph is still good. Pat and I talked Tuesday about the room setup and settled on the horseshoe — Pat was right, I was overthinking the forced quality of it, thirty people is not so many that a horseshoe reads as staged. The potential person in the afternoon window is apparently confirmed now, which I'm treating as just another fact about the room.

I'm going to try to carry Dani's sentence into the room with me, or not carry it exactly, but know it's there. That I sound like myself again. That the again is real and also, in some specific ways, costs something to sit with.

The dog came in from the yard and settled under the table, his usual spot. The house is still quiet. I should open the document — one more pass, the maintenance kind — and then let it rest until Saturday.

entries.prompt: What's the last time you disagreed with Jamie about something — not a fight, just a moment where you wanted different things? Walk me through how it played out and what you didn't say.
entries.content:
The second coffee is worse than the first because I made it distracted, miscounted something. It's fine. I'm drinking it anyway.

This was three days ago, Sunday evening, the house doing its Sunday-evening thing where the weekend is clearly ending and everyone is performing a last few hours of not thinking about Monday. Jamie and I were in the kitchen after dinner, the kids upstairs, the dog under the table, the good quiet that happens when nothing is required of you for twenty minutes. And Jamie said, without a lot of preamble, that maybe we should think about getting away for a few days after the workshop. Just the two of us. A long weekend somewhere, anywhere, just out of this house and not thinking about any of it for a little while.

I felt two things at once, which seems to be my default configuration.

The first thing was: yes, obviously yes, that sounds exactly right, we have not done that in long enough that I can't immediately name the last time, and Jamie has been managing more of the household machinery for nine months and asking for almost nothing and a few days away is the most reasonable thing anyone has said to me in weeks.

The second thing, which arrived about half a second behind the first and quieter but insistent: not yet.

Not yet, because the workshop ends and then there's the debrief with Pat, and the follow-up I'm already planning to the outreach reply, and the potential person from the afternoon window who might turn into something, and the thing I've been imagining for two weeks is not just the room clearing and the coats — it's also what comes after, whether a door opens, whether the email arrives, whether the morning three days later feels different from the morning now. I want to be present for that. I want to be in the house and at the table and available when the thing either starts to become something or doesn't.

I didn't say any of that. I said it sounded really good, let's figure something out.

Jamie started talking about possible timing, a few options, places we'd mentioned before and never gotten to. And I was nodding and engaged and also not entirely there, doing the thing where part of me is in the conversation and part of me is already in the two weeks after the workshop, counting whether the thing I've been building is finally going to show me something definitive. Jamie was talking about a particular place we'd discussed a few years ago and I was thinking about the potential follow-up email I haven't written yet.

Which is its own situation.

Here's what I didn't say, and why I'm still thinking about it: Jamie is not wrong. A few days away is a good idea. The exhaustion that has been accumulating in this house for nine months is real and most of it has fallen on Jamie's side of the ledger, and I know that, and a long weekend somewhere is a small fraction of what would actually balance that account. The not-yet is real too, but the not-yet is mine. It's about what I need from the next two weeks. It has nothing to do with what Jamie has earned from the same period.

The voice: *you are planning to defer something reasonable because you are not ready to stop watching for the result, and you are doing it by agreeing instead of saying so.*

Yes. Correct. I said it sounded really good and meant it and also meant not yet and only said one of those things.

I've been wondering since Sunday what I was actually protecting. The obvious answer is the post-workshop window, the emails and the follow-ups and not wanting to be two hours away in a rental cottage when something arrives that requires a response. But I think there's something under that, which is that going away with Jamie right after the workshop means being somewhere where the only thing on offer is the two of us, and that is genuinely something I want, and also the workshop is still in front of me and the question of what it proves or doesn't prove is still open, and I think I would rather know the answer to that question before I sit across from Jamie at a table somewhere without the work between us.

That's the uncomfortable version. The work has been a kind of privacy. I have been inside it in a specific way this year — the document, the early mornings, the imagined room — and Jamie has been steady and present and on the outside of most of it. A few days away would be the inside-version. No document, no mornings with the laptop open, no the kids need something. Just: us, and who we are with each other when nothing else is providing structure.

I want that. I've been wanting it for longer than I've said. But wanting it means being available to the version of the conversation where Jamie asks, finally, how it's actually going — not the dinner-table version, not the careful updates, but the full version, two in the morning somewhere, the one where I say the thing about the outreach document with seven versions and the gap between what I can describe and what I can demonstrate and the spreadsheet that opens at eleven on quiet nights. And I don't know if I want to have that conversation before the workshop or after, when the answer is at least partly different than it is right now.

We haven't nailed down any dates. Jamie mentioned it once more on Monday, casually, and I said let's lock something in after the workshop, when I have more clarity on what the next few weeks look like. Jamie said sure, made a note somewhere, moved on. No friction. Jamie is very good at not making things into friction.

Which is its own kind of pressure, if I'm honest. It would be easier if there were something to push against.

The dog is asking for his walk. The paper on the table has, somehow, gotten worse — a folder I opened Tuesday and didn't close added itself to the pile and now there's an avalanche situation threatening the left side. I'm going to deal with it this week. I've been saying this for two months and I'm going to say it again now with the same level of conviction.

Five days. The workshop is in five days. After the workshop I'm going to book the thing with Jamie, somewhere specific, a date I can't move, and I'm going to try to be the version of myself who shows up to it without the document open in the back of my head. I don't know if I'll manage it. But I want to.

I should have said not yet and then explained the not-yet. That was what was true on Sunday. I said yes instead because yes was easier and because I didn't want to have to explain the part about waiting to see what the workshop proves. That's still sitting there.

The light outside is flat and bright, the particular November light that has no warmth to it but a lot of clarity. I can see every detail of the yard. The fence, the oak that made all the leaves I raked, the bare line of the back property where the dog is standing now, looking at the spot where he always expects the squirrel.

The squirrel is not there.

He checks anyway.

entries.prompt: What's something you're dreading in the next week or two — not a catastrophe, just a thing that's been sitting in the back of your mind? Walk me through what you're imagining when you let yourself think about it, and what version of you shows up in that imagining.
entries.content:
The coffee is right this morning, which feels like a small act of goodwill from the universe, or at least from my own distracted hands finally paying attention.

Four days.

I've been carrying the thing about Dani's husband for a week and haven't written it down yet. That's not what's sitting heaviest. What's sitting heaviest is something I haven't let myself look at straight on, the way you don't look directly at a bright light — just peripheral, there and there and there, every time the workshop comes up in a concrete way. The room. The horseshoe. Pat on one side, me on the other. Thirty people who came for something.

What I'm dreading is the specific possibility that I will be good but not as good as Pat.

Not a disaster. Not freezing, not losing the room, not the kind of failure you could point to and say that's what went wrong. I know how to be competent in a room. I did it for long enough, in enough different configurations, that competence is almost certainly the floor. What I can't guarantee — what I have not been able to stop imagining in the unguarded moments, the loading-the-dishwasher moments — is the version where Pat handles something I don't. A question from the audience that lands in Pat's half of the room and Pat catches it cleanly, makes something out of it, and I watch from across the horseshoe and know that I would have handled it adequately but Pat handled it actually well. And the thirty people see that too, the way thirty people always see that, even without saying anything about it.

Pat has been doing this for three years. I've been doing this for nine months and two workshops, one of which I described as fine while fine was doing a lot of lifting. I know these numbers. I've known them since I said yes to this in the driveway.

The version of me that shows up in the imagining is trying too hard. That's the clearest way I can say it. When I let myself into the dread — when I follow it into the specifics — I can see a version of myself watching Pat handle the question well and then overcorrecting, stepping in on the next thing more forcefully than I would have naturally, pushing the language a little further than the moment needs. Trying to rebalance something the room hasn't noticed is unbalanced. Which is the thing you're not supposed to do and which I know not to do and which I'm genuinely afraid I will do anyway, because knowing and doing have never been the same thing for me in the heat of it.

*You have been preparing for weeks and you are now preparing for the possibility that the preparing won't be enough, which is a different kind of preparing.*

The walk yesterday, flat November light, the dog pulling toward the creek. Yes. That's what this is.

There's a cleaner version of the dread that's easier to say, which is just: what if something goes wrong in a technical sense, what if the material doesn't land, what if the room has an energy that resists what we've built. That version I can plan against, and I have, and it sits in the document. But the specific dread — the one I'm up early with, the one I haven't said to Pat or Jamie or anyone — is about the side-by-side. About being visible next to someone who has more of this than I do and having thirty people witness the comparison.

Which tells me something I don't love about why I agreed to this in the first place. Not the whole story, I've been over the whole story, both things were true and I still think the yes was right. But there's a strand of the yes that was about proving something in proximity to Pat, and the dread is that same strand running in the other direction. What if the proximity proves the wrong thing.

I should have told Pat more of this before now. We had the conversation in the loud coffee place, the ceiling that turned out to be a ceiling, and that was real, and I let myself believe the openness was more complete than it actually was. There are rooms I don't open even with Pat. This is one of them.

The potential person from the afternoon window being confirmed made it worse, not better. I've been thinking about that since it happened. Because now if the thing I'm afraid of happens — the visible gap, the handling-it-well — it doesn't just happen in front of thirty people. It happens in front of someone who might be deciding whether to hire one of us or both of us or neither. The imagined room I've been keeping close, the warm version, the coats and the someone finding their way to me — I can't access that image as cleanly as I could two weeks ago. Something has gotten into it.

*You are imagining the outcome that confirms your worst read of the evidence.*

I know. I know that's what I'm doing. The pat on the second block, the third paragraph that held up on re-reading, Jamie saying more like yourself, Dani's sentence that I've been turning over for a week. The evidence runs both directions and I know which direction I'm weighting this morning.

But also: the dread is information. It's telling me something about where the stakes actually live, which is not in the material or the arc or even the introduction, which is settled and right. The stakes live in being seen. Being seen next to Pat, being seen by the room, being seen by the potential-person-in-the-afternoon, and finding out in real time whether the thing I've been building has gotten solid enough to hold the weight of being looked at.

That's what I've been waiting nine months to find out. And finding out requires being in the room, which requires walking through the specific dread of possibly finding out the answer is not yet.

I'm going to drive over to the venue today. Pat mentioned the organizer said we could have a look at the setup before Thursday, and I said I might, and I've been vaguely planning it since. But what I want from the room — the actual room, not the composite I've been building from other rooms — is just to stand in it once before it has people in it. See where the light comes from. Count the chairs. Let the space be real before it's full.

Nina came down while I was in the middle of this and asked if we had any of those crackers left. I said yes, top shelf. She found them, looked at me for a second, and said, out of nothing, "you look worried."

I said I'm fine, just thinking about something.

She said: "is it the workshop."

I said yes.

She chewed a cracker. Then: "it's going to be fine. You're good at talking to people."

She said it the way she says things, complete and facing forward and already moving on. Got her glass of water and went back upstairs.

I sat here for a minute.

She's eleven and she said it like it was obvious and it landed somewhere, briefly, before the dread settled back in around it. But it was there. The landing.

Four days. I should go see the room.

entries.prompt: What's something a kid in your life did or said recently that caught you off guard — walk me through the moment and what it stirred up.
entries.content:
The walk this morning was longer than I meant it to be. The dog found something in the drainage ditch at the far end of the road and wouldn't be moved for a while, and I stood there in the cold and let him investigate, and by the time we turned back the light had changed from dark-gray to light-gray and the day had technically started without me consenting to it.

Three days.

I went to see the room yesterday. Drove over, met the organizer briefly, walked around for fifteen minutes while she answered emails on her phone by the door. It's a good room. Better than the composite I'd built — windows on two sides, not one, and the light in the afternoon is going to come from an angle that actually helps, that does the thing good light does in a room where people are supposed to be paying attention. I counted the chairs. Stood in the spot where I'll be standing Thursday and looked at where the horseshoe will open and thought: okay. Okay.

I don't know if it helped. I think it helped.

I came home and Nina was at the kitchen table with a project. Not the poster-or-model thing from a few weeks ago — something new, a different assignment, some kind of written thing with a component she was clearly stuck on. She had three different colored pens arranged in a way that meant something to her, and she was staring at the paper the way she stares at things when she's trying to make them cooperate through force of concentration alone.

I made a sandwich, sat down at the other end of the table. The paper situation is slightly improved since Tuesday when I finally dealt with most of the left side — the avalanche has been contained, there's actual visible table between us.

She worked for a few minutes without saying anything, which for Nina is its own kind of signal, that she's in it enough that the filtering is turned down. Then she looked up and said: "what do you do when you don't know how to start something."

I thought she was asking about the assignment. I started to say something about just writing one sentence, any sentence, because the starting is different from the doing, and you can always change the first sentence later—

She said: "no, I know that. I meant like in general. What do you actually do."

I stopped.

She was looking at me with the specific quality of attention she has when she's asking the real question, not the surface one. Chin up slightly. Pen in hand but not moving. She looked — I want to be accurate about this — she looked like she was consulting someone. Like I was a source she'd decided to check.

I said: I start anyway, and I feel bad about it for a while, and then usually something happens that's better than if I'd waited until I was ready.

She thought about this. Chewed the end of the pen, which is a thing I've told her not to do and have stopped telling her not to do.

Then she said: "is that what you're doing with the workshop thing? Starting even though you don't know."

I had to sit with that for a second.

The honest answer is that the workshop is not a start-anyway situation, not anymore — the prep is done, the arc holds, the introduction is two sentences that tell the truth and stop, I went and stood in the room and counted the chairs. I'm as ready as I'm going to be. But nine months ago, the whole shape of this year, the day I sat in this kitchen and thought about what came next: yes. That's exactly what that was. Starting anyway, feeling bad about it for a while, waiting to see if something happens that's better than if I'd waited.

I said: basically, yeah.

She nodded slowly, like she was filing it. Then she looked back down at her paper and picked up the green pen, and within about ninety seconds she was writing. Not tentatively, actually writing, the fast version.

I sat there for a while with my sandwich and watched her work.

What it stirred up — and I've been turning it over since yesterday afternoon, all through making dinner, loading the dishwasher, the flat gray hour before bed — is something I don't have clean language for yet. Some combination of: she was asking me something real and I answered it without preparing for it, and the answer was true, and she used it. Those things in that sequence.

I've been so aware of the gap between what I can describe and what I can demonstrate. Nine months of it. The outreach document with seven versions, the pitch that's slightly ahead of the proof, the calls that end with let me think about it. The evidence question, which has been running underneath everything. And Nina asked me what I do when I don't know how to start something, and I told her what's actually true, and she went back to her work.

That's demonstration. Small, eleven-year-old-at-a-kitchen-table demonstration. But she asked me as someone who might have useful information, and I did, and she used it. That's a different thing than I've been tracking on the spreadsheet.

The other thing — the one I didn't see coming and that landed somewhere I wasn't expecting — is that she already knew the answer to her own question. She knows how to start things. She starts things all the time, loudly, without apparent difficulty. The question was something else. I think she was checking whether I was living inside the same problem she was, whether her father, who has been sitting at this table for nine months with a document open and an expression that apparently reads as worried, was doing the thing or avoiding the thing. And when I said basically yeah, that's what I'm doing — she accepted it and went back to work.

She wasn't worried about whether the answer was good enough. She just needed to know it was honest.

*She was checking whether you were still in it.*

That came up on the walk this morning, the dog finally satisfied with the drainage ditch, both of us heading home in the gray light. Whether I was still in it. Whether the starting-anyway was ongoing or had stalled somewhere into the perpetual almost-starting that she's been watching for nine months. I think she decided yes, still in it, based on nothing more than the way I answered a question over a sandwich.

Theo would have asked differently, if he'd asked at all. His version would have come at an angle, in the car, in the half-sentences of someone who has decided to trust the conversation but doesn't want to seem like he's decided. This was Nina's version: direct, at the table, with the green pen already in her hand.

Jamie had a late meeting last night and came home tired, and I told her Nina had asked me something unexpected and I'd managed to answer it without embarrassing myself. Jamie laughed — the good laugh, the one that's been a little rarer this year with the extra orchestrating falling on her side — and said Nina is going to be alarming when she's thirty.

I said: she's already a little alarming.

Three days. The room has good light. Nina went back to her project and finished it, or enough of it that she closed the folder with the decisive snap she does when a thing has been dealt with.

That snap. I'm going to try to carry that into Thursday with me.

entries.prompt: The workshop is three days behind you now. What actually happened in the room — not the version you've been telling people, but the version you've been replaying privately?
entries.content:
The coffee is from yesterday's grounds, technically, because I forgot to set up the machine last night and this morning I couldn't face starting from scratch. It's fine. It's coffee.

I've been sitting here for an hour and I haven't opened the laptop.

The version I've been telling people: it went well. Really well, actually. The room had good energy, Pat and I found our rhythm, some things landed that I'd hoped would land. All true. I've said it to Jamie, I said it to Dani who texted Sunday, I said it to Mom when she called yesterday afternoon — she'd been waiting, I could feel it in the first two seconds of the call, the particular held quality of someone who has been hoping for news. I said it went really well and she made the sound and I felt it in my chest the way I always feel it, the relief-and-something-else.

Here's the version I've been replaying.

The room was better than the composite I'd built. The light did what I'd hoped. Thirty-one people ended up in the horseshoe, one more than expected, and the extra chair was from the morning's confirmed addition — the potential person from the afternoon window who turned out to be there from the start, which I hadn't known, which I found out when I was looking at the filled horseshoe at nine-fifteen and Pat leaned over and said quietly, *third from the left, gray jacket.* I nodded and moved on and told myself it was just another fact about the room.

The first block went the way prepared things go when you've prepared them enough: clean but slightly sealed. I could feel the material working, could feel the room following, and I could also feel myself inside the language in a way that was competent and not yet alive. Like I was driving a route I knew too well. Pat handled the opening differently — looser, a question to the room in the first three minutes that I hadn't expected and wouldn't have done, and the room opened a little. I watched it happen. Filed it.

Midway through the second block something changed. There was a question from someone — I hadn't caught her name in the introductions, forties, sat very still the whole morning — and the question was not quite what the material had prepared me for. It wasn't hostile, wasn't off-topic. It was just more specific than I was ready for, pointed at a thing I believe but haven't had to defend out loud in front of thirty people and a gray jacket. A version of: yes, but how do you actually know that.

I felt the moment land. Not panic — something colder than panic, more like a sudden clarity about where I was and what was being asked. And I heard the dread from Monday, the imagined version, the overcorrecting version, and I made a decision somewhere below the level of conscious decision-making, which was: don't answer yet. Ask her something first.

I asked what had made the question come up for her, what she was working with that was making it feel unresolved.

She talked for about forty-five seconds. The room listened. And while she was talking I found the answer — not the prepared version, the actual version, the one that comes from nine months of sitting at a table and grinding through the gap between what I can describe and what I can demonstrate. I said it. It wasn't long. It was maybe four sentences, and the last one was something I hadn't planned and couldn't have planned, it just arrived.

She nodded. The room was quiet for a second in the way that means something landed, not in the way that means something went wrong.

Pat caught my eye from across the horseshoe. Just for a second. Something passed between us that I don't have a word for. Acknowledgment, maybe. Or just two people who've been preparing for six weeks looking at each other across a room and knowing: this is what we came for.

That's the moment I've been replaying.

Not what came after — the afternoon block, which was good, maybe better than the morning, Pat doing a thing with a small group exercise that pulled the room fully open in a way I hadn't seen coming. Not the end, which was messier than I'd imagined, people leaving in clusters rather than lingering, fewer one-on-ones than I'd built the imagined scene around. Not the gray jacket, who did find me at the end, briefly, and said something about being in touch — careful, professional, nothing I can bank on. The debrief with Pat in the parking lot for twenty minutes, both of us tired and running on the particular fuel of having done the thing, saying things I'll want to remember when I'm back to normal speed.

What I keep returning to is those four sentences. The fact that they came from the part of me that has been sitting at this table for nine months and not from the part that spent six weeks preparing a deck.

*You needed nine months of preparation to answer a question that six weeks of preparation couldn't have answered.*

That arrived on the walk Saturday morning, two days after, the dog pulling toward the drainage ditch again and me letting him. Not triumphant. More like something clicking into place after a long time of not quite fitting.

I called Pat Saturday afternoon. We talked for almost an hour. The potential follow-up with the gray jacket, which Pat has more context on — they know the company, had a sense of what they were looking for coming in. Pat was careful about it, realistic, said don't count anything yet. I said I know. We talked about what worked and what didn't, the honest version, the kind of conversation I wasn't sure we were capable of before we'd actually been in a room together. We are capable of it. That was good to find out.

At dinner Thursday night — the same night, still running on whatever it was — Nina asked how it went. I said really well. She said what happened. And I tried to tell her the version a child could follow, the question from the woman, asking her something first, the four sentences. Nina listened with the chin-up quality she has.

She said: see.

I said: yeah.

Theo, from his end of the table, without looking up from his food: "told you."

He hadn't told me anything. He'd said hope it goes well, one time, on his way upstairs. But he'd been watching for nine months and I think he'd arrived at his own version of told you, assembled from something other than what I'd said out loud, and he offered it matter-of-factly the way he offers things, and then went back to his dinner.

I went back to mine.

The paper situation on the table is worse again — I can't account for it, it's like the paper generates itself overnight — but there's a cleared corner where the coffee mug is sitting right now, and the morning is quiet, and I haven't opened the laptop yet.

The follow-up to the outreach person needs to go out today. The gray jacket is somewhere in the future, or isn't — I can't know yet and I'm trying to leave it there. The spreadsheet is the same spreadsheet it's been for nine months, the math still coming out fine, and I opened it last night out of habit and then closed it faster than usual, which might mean something.

The four sentences were mine. I made them in that room from what I actually know, not from what I know how to describe. The difference between those two things was the whole problem, or most of it, and it's not solved, but the distance between them is narrower than it was Thursday morning when I walked into a room with good light and counted thirty-one chairs.

That's what actually happened.

The coffee, even from yesterday's grounds, is fine.

entries.prompt: What's the last time you lied by omission — not to protect someone else, but to protect yourself? Walk me through the moment: what was actually happening, what you left out, and what you were hoping they wouldn't ask.
entries.content:
The grounds this morning are fresh, the machine reset. Small victory. I'm on the first cup and it's still hot enough to matter.

Dani called Wednesday. Short call, checking in, the warm-and-careful version we do. She asked how the workshop had gone — she'd texted after and I'd sent back something brief, good, more soon, which Dani accepted without pushing — and I told her the version I've been telling people. The room, the energy, Pat and I finding our rhythm. All true. I gave her the moment with the question, the woman who sat very still, the four sentences, because Dani deserved more than the one-line version and I had enough distance from it by Wednesday to tell it without feeling like I was taking something out of my pocket to show her.

She was glad. Genuinely glad, I could hear it. She said: *that's the thing right there, that's the whole thing.*

And then she asked: *so what's next?*

I said the follow-ups were underway, some interesting conversations on the horizon, I was thinking about the shape of the next few months. All of which is true in the sense that I have thought about those things. Spent time with them. Have a loose structure in my head, some language for it, the way you have language for a thing before the thing is real.

What I didn't say: I don't know.

Not the performed version of I don't know, the humble-brag version that means *I have so many options I haven't chosen yet.* The actual version. The gray jacket said something careful and professional and Pat has more context than I do and I can't bank on anything. The outreach follow-up went out and hasn't come back yet. The potential conversations are potential, which is not the same thing as conversations, and I've been in this long enough to know the distance between those two words. I am nine months and one workshop in and I still don't know exactly what the next thing is.

I didn't say that because Dani had just received the good version of the story and I didn't want to put a complicated thing right up against it. That's what I told myself.

The more accurate version is that I didn't say it because Dani's question — *so what's next* — had the quality of an opening I could either step into or move past, and I moved past, and moving past was easier than saying the thing that's actually true, which is: I don't know what's next and I'm trying not to let the not-knowing eat the thing that just happened, but some mornings it's eating it anyway.

She wouldn't have been alarmed. That's what I keep coming back to this morning, the part that makes the omission sit sideways. Dani is not Mom. Dani doesn't have the particular frequency that turns my uncertainty into something she has to carry. She's across the country, she's in her own complicated middle of things, and she called me a month ago from inside a decision she didn't know how to make and I mostly listened and asked the right questions and let her find her own way to it. She would have done the same. She is actually capable of sitting in the I don't know with me without converting it into a worry she needs to resolve.

And I gave her the version with the shape instead.

*You dressed it up because you wanted her to see it as more finished than it is. Not for her. For you.*

That came in on the walk this morning, the dog finally uninterested in the drainage ditch, both of us just moving. And it's right. There's a version of the truth I've been offering people since Thursday that is accurate and also curated in a specific direction — toward the thing being more landed than it actually is. The four sentences, which are real. The room with good light, which was real. *Really well, actually,* which I believed when I said it and still believe and also which does not include the part where I came home Thursday night and sat in the kitchen after everyone was asleep and felt the particular comedown of having done a thing you've been building toward and not yet knowing what it was for.

Not disappointment. Not that. More like the specific quiet of having crossed a finish line that turned out to be a waypoint. The imagined room — the coats, the someone finding their way to me, the thing that landed — some of that happened and some of it didn't, and I haven't done the accounting yet in any honest way, I've just been telling people the version that doesn't require accounting.

I would have said something different to Pat. I have said something different to Pat. Saturday's hour-long call, the honest version, the what worked and what didn't. But Pat is in the same thing. Pat has a stake in seeing it clearly. Everyone else gets the told-you version, the really well version, the it's going well momentum version I gave my mother.

And Dani got something in between, which might be the most dishonest of all, because Dani asked a real question and I gave her a shaped answer, and she received it as the real answer, and now she's across the country thinking I have some sense of what comes next when the truth is I'm sitting at this table with the cleared corner and the fresh grounds and the paper that generates itself overnight and I am doing the same accounting I have been doing for nine months, just with one data point added.

One good data point. I want to be clear about that. The four sentences, the room, Theo's *told you* said to his plate. Those are real. They go in the column that exists and I'm not moving them.

But Dani asked what's next and I said the shapes of things instead of I don't know, and I was protecting something. The sense that I have some handle on the momentum. The version of myself that is further along than the actual version, the one who has clarity about the next quarter rather than a loose structure and some language. I wanted her to see that version. And because she loves me and was glad the workshop went well and asked a friendly question rather than a pointed one, she saw whatever I showed her.

I'm going to call her back this week. Not to correct the record formally, that would be strange. Just to actually answer the question. Say I've been thinking about what's next and I don't have it figured out yet and here's what the not-figured-out actually looks like, specifically, in the real accounting version.

She'll be fine with it. She'll probably say something useful.

The gray jacket is where I left it, in the future or not. The follow-up is out and I'm not checking. The spreadsheet opened last night and closed faster than usual and this morning I'm leaving it closed.

Jamie comes downstairs in twenty minutes, give or take. I'm going to try to say something true at the table before the day starts organizing itself. Not the full version, not a monologue. Just one sentence that's actually in the real register.

I haven't decided which sentence yet.

entries.prompt: What's something you own that you've been meaning to get rid of for a long time but keep finding reasons not to? Walk me through the last time it caught your eye — what you told yourself, and why it's still there.
entries.content:
The conference mug is still on the table.

I moved it twice this week — once to the left side of the cleared corner when I was making room for the laptop, once to the windowsill above the sink when I ran out of places to put things during the paper situation. Both times I put it down without thinking about it, the way you handle objects that have been in the same space long enough to become ambient. It's been on the windowsill since Wednesday, which means it's been catching the morning light in a way that makes it more visible than it's been in months, and this morning I stood at the sink filling the kettle and looked at it for probably thirty seconds.

The mug is from a conference four years ago. I said this before, weeks ago, and said I'd been meaning to throw it away. It is still here.

I should say what it is, which is not a particularly nice mug. Cheap ceramic, the kind that comes in a tote bag with a lanyard and a branded notepad. The logo on the side is for a conference in a field adjacent to what I used to do, not even my specific corner of it — I'd gone because a colleague was presenting and it was a reasonable excuse to be in the city for a night. I don't remember much about the conference itself. I remember the hotel was fine. I remember the dinner with the colleague ran long in a way that felt important at the time and doesn't anymore.

The mug came home in my bag because I was too tired to leave it in the hotel room and too distracted to throw it away at the airport.
